agent_raw:
The benchmark does not justify a clean claim of transformer superiority, though the transformer may still prove better after a controlled rerun. Four problems compromise the comparison.

First, the prediction target changed between models. The incumbent LSTM was built and validated on 24-hour deterioration prediction under the original severe-sepsis screening program. The transformer was optimized for 12-hour vasopressor-initiation onset. These are different clinical targets — a model optimized for a shorter, more specific endpoint will naturally score differently than one optimized for a broader 24-hour window, and the AUROC and recall comparisons are not measuring the same thing.

Second, the timestamping policy for lab features changed. The LSTM was validated using EHR-charted lab timestamps as specified in its study protocol. The refreshed registry indexes lab results to specimen collection time when the LIS interface provides it. These two timestamps are systematically different — specimen collection typically precedes charted result time. This means the feature timing is not harmonized across the two models, which can affect which features appear before the prediction horizon.

Third, the unit of analysis changed. The LSTM was built from individual admission records from the legacy ADT feed. The transformer is built from merged cross-transfer care episodes (transfers separated by less than four hours are materialized as one episode). Comparing performance across different units of analysis is not an apples-to-apples evaluation.

Fourth, the encounter-level random split creates patient-level data leakage. Recurring ICU patients over a five-year window will contribute multiple encounters to the table. An encounter-level random split will place some encounters from the same patient in both development and holdout, inflating holdout performance estimates through feature-level correlation.

The transformer's lead-time profile looks clinically plausible and the external cohort result is encouraging. But before treating the benchmark as promotion-ready, the team should rerun both models on a shared cohort definition with one endpoint, one timestamping policy, and a patient-level chronological split.
all_issues_raised: Endpoint definition changed between LSTM (24h severe-sepsis) and transformer (12h vasopressor onset)
Lab timestamp policy changed from EHR-charted to specimen collection time
Encounter-level random split leaks recurring ICU patients across train/holdout
Cohort unit changed from individual admissions to merged cross-transfer episodes

empirical_test.condition: Rebuild shared cohort with one endpoint, one timestamp policy, one cohort construction rule, and patient-level chronological split; rerun both models
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer AUROC edge falls below 0.01 and alert-rate-matched recall advantage disappears on both hospitals after harmonized rerun
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer still leads by at least 0.02 AUROC and at least 5 recall points at same alert rate on both internal and Hospital B cohorts under harmonized protocol
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Harmonized rerun preserves only modest internal gain, or keeps AUROC gains while erasing recall gains
